title: The Role of Intuition in Business Decisions
meta_description: Trust your gut in business. Data has blind spots. Intuition fills them. Learn to use both for better decisions.
og_title: The Role of Intuition in Business Decisions
og_description: Trust your gut in business. Data has blind spots. Intuition fills them. Learn to use both for better decisions.
body:
You Already Know the Answer. You Just Don't Trust It Yet.

Let me guess. You've been in a meeting where everyone around the table was looking at spreadsheets, data projections, and cost-benefit analyses. And while they were talking, you felt something in your gut. A tightness. A pull. A quiet "no" that had nothing to do with the numbers. But you didn't say anything. Because how do you explain a gut feeling to a room full of people who think intuition is just a fancy word for guessing?

I'm Kalesh, and I've been where you are. You're not broken. Your nervous system isn't misfiring. You're just calibrated for a wider range than most people. And when it comes to business decisions, that calibration is your secret weapon, not your weakness.

Here's the thing nobody tells you about business: the data is never complete. Every spreadsheet has blind spots. Every projection is built on assumptions that might break tomorrow. And your intuition? That's the part of you that's already reading the room, sensing the shift in energy, and picking up on the patterns your conscious mind hasn't caught yet. It's not magic. It's pattern recognition running on a faster processor than your rational brain can handle.

"Intuition isn't the opposite of logic. It's the part of your intelligence that processes information too fast for your conscious mind to catch up. In business, it's the difference between making a decision and making the right one."

Why Your Gut Feelings Are Actually Business Intelligence

Think about the last time you made a decision that turned out to be exactly right, but you couldn't explain why. Maybe you hired someone who everyone else thought was a risk, and they turned out to be your best employee. Maybe you passed on a deal that looked perfect on paper, but something felt off. You couldn't articulate it at the time, but you were right.

That's not luck. That's your intuitive system doing what it does best. Your nervous system picks up on micro-expressions, tonal shifts, environmental cues, and energetic signals that your rational brain filters out. It's like having a backstage pass to the reality of a situation while everyone else is watching the show from their seats.

And here's the part that might sting a little: the reason you've been told your whole life that something is wrong with you is because you're operating on a frequency most people can't hear. You're picking up on the static in the room, the unspoken tensions, the opportunities that haven't announced themselves yet. In a world that values certainty and data, that sensitivity can feel like a liability. But in business, it's a superpower.

Right?! You've felt that moment when you knew something was going to happen before it did, and you didn't say anything because you didn't want to look foolish. That's the wound talking. The Bright Wound. The part of you that's been burned for being too sensitive, too perceptive, too much. But that wound is also your doorway to seeing what others miss.

The Myth of the Rational Business Leader

We've been sold a story that good business decisions come from cold, hard logic. That the best leaders are the ones who can set aside their feelings and just look at the numbers. But here's the truth: every major business success story I've ever studied has a moment where someone ignored the data and followed their gut. Steve Jobs didn't launch the iPhone because of market research. He launched it because he knew what people wanted before they knew it themselves.

Does that land? Because I think you know exactly what I'm talking about. You've had those moments where the "rational" choice felt wrong, and the intuitive choice felt scary. And when you followed the intuitive one, it worked. But then you second-guessed yourself the next time, because that's what we do when we've been trained to distrust our own knowing.

The problem isn't that intuition is unreliable. The problem is that we've never been taught how to use it properly. We treat intuition like a mystical gift that either works or doesn't, instead of treating it like a muscle that needs to be trained and calibrated. You wouldn't expect to bench press 200 pounds without ever lifting a weight. So why do you expect your intuition to be perfect without practice?

How to Tell the Difference Between Intuition and Fear

This is the question I get asked more than any other. "Kalesh, how do I know if it's my intuition talking or just my anxiety?" And it's a good question, because they can feel the same way in your body. Both can give you a tight chest, a churning stomach, a sense of urgency. But here's the key difference: fear contracts, intuition expands.

When you're making a decision from fear, your body feels tight, closed off, and protective. You're looking for safety. You're trying to avoid a negative outcome. Your mind races with worst-case scenarios. You feel small and defensive.

When you're making a decision from intuition, your body feels open, calm, and clear. Even if the decision is scary (and it often is), there's a sense of rightness underneath it. You feel expanded. You're moving toward something, not running away from something. The knowing is quiet but unshakeable.

Try this next time you're at a crossroads: take three deep breaths. Slow them down. Then ask yourself, "If I knew for certain that nothing bad would happen, what would I choose?" The answer that comes up first? That's your intuition. The fear comes in when you start thinking about all the ways it could go wrong.

Practical Ways to Use Intuition in Business Without Looking Crazy

Look, I get it. You can't walk into a board meeting and say, "My gut says we should pivot." You need a bridge between the intuitive knowing and the rational justification. Here's how you build that bridge.

Start with small decisions. You don't need to bet the company on a hunch. Start using your intuition for low-stakes choices: which email to reply to first, which vendor to call back, which meeting to reschedule. Pay attention to how it feels when you're right. Build trust with yourself on the small stuff before you take it to the big stuff.

Give your intuition a voice in the room. When you're in a meeting and something feels off, you don't have to say "my intuition says..." Instead, try: "I have a concern that I can't fully justify with the data yet, but I think we should look at this from another angle." You're honoring the intuitive hit without making it sound woo-woo. You're giving yourself permission to explore the feeling without having to defend it.

Create space for quiet. Your intuition can't speak if you're constantly drowning it out with noise. Schedule five minutes of silence before every major decision. Close your eyes, breathe, and ask yourself: "What do I already know about this situation that I'm not letting myself see?" The answers will surprise you.

Keep an intuition journal. Before you make a decision, write down what your gut is telling you. Then come back a week later and see if you were right. This isn't about being perfect. It's about calibrating your instrument. Over time, you'll start to see patterns. You'll learn which kinds of situations your intuition is strongest in, and where you need more data.

For a deeper dive into how to train this muscle, I highly recommend Sonia Choquette's "The Answer Is Simple" - See on Amazon (paid link). She breaks down the practical steps for trusting your inner voice in a way that's grounded and real.

When Intuition and Data Collide (And What to Do About It)

This is where it gets tricky. You've got a spreadsheet that says one thing, and your gut that says another. What do you do?

First, check your assumptions. Is the data actually complete? Most data sets have blind spots. Maybe the market research was done on the wrong demographic. Maybe the projections are based on a model that doesn't account for a variable you're sensing. Your intuition might be picking up on something the data missed.

Second, check your intention. Are you ignoring the data because it's inconvenient? Or are you following your gut because you've seen this pattern before and you know how it ends? Be honest with yourself. If you're just avoiding hard truths, that's not intuition. That's denial.

Third, look for the third option. The choice isn't always "follow the data" or "follow your gut." Sometimes the best decision is to gather more information, or to run a small experiment that tests your intuitive hit without risking everything. Can you do a pilot program? Can you run a test campaign? Can you talk to one more person who might have the piece you're missing?

I've seen people make catastrophic mistakes by ignoring their intuition in favor of data that looked good on paper. And I've seen people make equally catastrophic mistakes by following their gut without any grounding in reality. The sweet spot is where intuition and data dance together. Your intuition gives you the direction. The data helps you build the bridge.

The Role of Sensitivity in Reading People and Situations

This is where your Bright Wound becomes your biggest asset. You feel things that other people don't. You pick up on the tension in the room before anyone says a word. You know when a client is about to walk away from a deal, even when they're nodding along in the meeting.

That sensitivity isn't a curse. It's a radar system. And in business, the people who can read the room, sense the shift, and act before the competition even notices are the ones who win. Think about the best negotiators you've ever seen. They're not the ones with the most data. They're the ones who can feel when the other side is about to fold, or when they need to walk away.

You've been trained your whole life to suppress this sensitivity because it was too much for other people. But in business, it's exactly what you need. The question isn't whether you have it. The question is whether you're going to let it work for you or against you.

If you want to go deeper into how to work with your sensitivity instead of against it, check out "The Highly Sensitive Person" by Elaine Aron - See on Amazon (paid link). It's a breakthrough for understanding why you feel so much and how to use it as a strength.

Building Trust With Your Own Knowing

Here's the hard truth: you're not going to get it right every time. You're going to have moments where you follow your gut and it leads you into a wall. That doesn't mean your intuition is broken. It means you're human. Every muscle has to fail sometimes to grow stronger.

The real work is building trust with yourself. And trust isn't built by being right all the time. It's built by showing up, making a decision, and then being honest about what happened. When you get it wrong, ask yourself: "What did I miss? Was there a signal I ignored? Was I actually following fear instead of intuition?" Then adjust and move on.

Trust is also built by celebrating the wins. When your intuition pays off, stop and acknowledge it. Say out loud, "I knew that. I trusted myself, and I was right." That might feel silly, but it's how you rewire the neural pathways that have been telling you for years that you can't trust yourself.

You've been gaslit by a culture that doesn't understand sensitivity. You've been told to doubt yourself when you were actually seeing clearly. The healing starts when you stop apologizing for knowing what you know.

Practical Exercises to Calibrate Your Intuition for Business

Let me give you three things you can do starting today.

1. The Three-Question Pause. Before any decision, ask yourself: "What do I feel about this? What do I think about this? What do I know about this?" Feel is your emotional response. Think is your rational analysis. Know is your intuitive hit. Write down all three. Then compare them. Over time, you'll start to see which one is most reliable in different situations.

2. The Red Light/Green Light Check. When you're considering an option, close your eyes and imagine saying "yes" to it. Notice what happens in your body. Does it feel like a green light (open, expansive, calm) or a red light (tight, constricted, agitated)? Your body doesn't lie. It's giving you information your brain might miss.

3. The After-Action Review. At the end of every week, look back at the decisions you made. Which ones did you follow your intuition on? Which ones did you override it? What were the outcomes? This isn't about judgment. It's about pattern recognition. You're building a personal data set of when your intuition is strongest.

For a structured approach to this kind of inner work, "The Intuitive Way" by Penney Peirce - See on Amazon (paid link) is one of the best resources I've found. It walks you through the process step by step.

The Bottom Line: Your Sensitivity Is Your Edge

I'm going to be straight with you. The world of business is full of people who are disconnected from their own knowing. They make decisions based on spreadsheets and fear, and they wonder why they feel empty and anxious all the time. You have access to something they don't. You can feel the truth of a situation before it's visible to the naked eye.

That's not a weakness. That's a competitive advantage. But only if you use it.

The Bright Wound isn't something to heal so you can become "normal." It's something to work with so you can become who you were always meant to be. Your sensitivity, your intuition, your ability to feel what others can't - that's the gift. And in business, it's the thing that will set you apart from every other person in the room who's too busy looking at the numbers to see what's really happening.

So here's my challenge to you: the next time you have that quiet knowing, that pull in your gut, that sense that something is right or wrong - say something. Not loudly. Not aggressively. But with the quiet confidence of someone who knows they're onto something. You don't have to defend it. You just have to name it. And then watch what happens.

If you're ready to go even deeper into how to trust your inner voice and use it in every area of your life, "Trust Your Vibes" by Sonia Choquette - See on Amazon (paid link) is a fantastic resource. It's practical, grounded, and will give you the tools you need to stop second-guessing yourself.

You already know the answer. You've always known. The only question is whether you're ready to trust it.

Ready to take the next step? Take the free assessment to discover where your intuitive strengths lie and how to use them in your business decisions. Or try the quick quiz to see how your sensitivity is showing up right now. You're closer than you think.
